nodes.title: The Malice Box Quest
nodes.desc: The game invited readers to solve an escalating series of real and virtual puzzles across London:
nodes.body:
# The Malice Box Quest

An Alternate Reality Game (ARG) created by POKE London for Penguin Books to promote Martin Langfield's debut thriller novel *The Malice Box* — published by Michael Joseph (Penguin) on 28 February 2007. The game launched in early January 2007, approximately six weeks before publication, and ran for ~5 weeks. The publisher printed `www.maliceboxquest.com` directly in the book's first edition, signalling the ARG was integral to the publishing strategy from the outset.

---

## The Experience

The game invited readers to solve an escalating series of real and virtual puzzles across London:

1. **The Gridcracker** — An intricate spatial/logic puzzle online
2. **The Labyrinths** — Five increasingly difficult puzzles designed to eliminate casual players. One required converting visual patterns to braille to extract a phone number, which directed players to dial 10 digits as musical notes. Another referenced the Archimedes Principle as a puzzle element.
3. **The Real-World Climax** — The final seven contestants (the "Seven Ascendants") competed in a physical finale across London, culminating at the London Eye.

Navigation throughout used **Google Earth** (launched 2005, still genuinely novel in early 2007 — confirmed by SFF Chronicles community thread from Jan 10, 2007). A leaderboard with scoring tracked competitive play across an international community.

**Ultimate prize:** Aston Martin V8 Vantage + £10,000 cash.

Iain Tait's stated design goal: *"start off online with an immersive experience similar to an online game that becomes more and more real."*

---

## Reception

An active player community formed on the SFF Chronicles forum (confirmed thread: sffchronicles.com/threads/35047/, started Jan 10, 2007, running through Feb 13, 2007), with international participants from at least the UK and USA. Players from as far away as the US registered despite the UK-centric real-world puzzles. The ARG community likely also congregated on Unfiction forums (not yet retrieved from archives).

The novel was published in **13+ languages** (UK, US, German, Dutch, Italian, Spanish, Romanian, Indonesian, Turkish, Estonian, Polish, Portuguese editions confirmed), signalling high publisher confidence in the title — the ARG was part of a significant commercial launch push.

---

## Awards

No awards confirmed from research. Iain's recollection is "possibly some minor wins, nothing confirmed." No Cannes Lions, D&AD, Webby, Revolution, or NMA results found in open sources.

---

## Cultural Legacy

The Malice Box Quest is an early and sophisticated example of a commercial ARG in UK advertising — predating most publisher ARG campaigns by several years. Its combination of Google Earth navigation, braille puzzles, online community competition, and a physical London Eye finale placed it at the frontier of what the ARG community was calling "transmedia" storytelling. The novel's protagonist "blogs online" within the fiction, with the game site extending this into reality — an unusually deep cross-media integration for 2007.

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — Creative Director / ECD at POKE London (not the puzzle designer)
- **[Nik Roope](../collaborators/nik_roope.md)** — Co-Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Tim Wright](../collaborators/tim_wright.md)** — Scriptwriter (per crackunit.com blog archive)
- **[Simon Cook (Cookie)](../collaborators/cookie.md)** — POKE team contributor (per crackunit.com blog archive)
- **Martin Langfield** — Author of *The Malice Box* (Reuters East Coast Bureau Chief; first novel)
- **Penguin Books / Michael Joseph** — Client / Publisher
- *Puzzle designer: unconfirmed — role may overlap with Tim Wright, requires verification*
- *Producer, developer: not yet identified from open sources*

**POKE London team context (crackunit.com, Feb 2007):**
Yacco Vijn was announced as a new third Creative Director at POKE on 1 Feb 2007. Tom Hostler was MD ("Boss Hoss"). Asi Sharabi was in planning/strategy. It is not confirmed which of them worked on this specific project.

---

## References & Media

### Assets
![poke_malice_box img 01](../raw/media/poke_malice_box/poke_malice_box_img_01.jpg)
![poke_malice_box img 02](../raw/media/poke_malice_box/poke_malice_box_img_02.jpg)
![poke_malice_box img 03](../raw/media/poke_malice_box/poke_malice_box_img_03.jpg)
![poke_malice_box img 04](../raw/media/poke_malice_box/poke_malice_box_img_04.jpg)
![poke_malice_box img 05](../raw/media/poke_malice_box/poke_malice_box_img_05.jpg)

### The Book
- [Open Library: *The Malice Box* — Michael Joseph/Penguin first edition (2007), includes "Visit www.maliceboxquest.com"](https://openlibrary.org/books/OL39491501M/The_malice_box)
- [Penguin.co.uk product page (ebook/paperback edition)](https://www.penguin.co.uk/books/55641/the-malice-box-by-martin-langfield/9780141909400)

### Community Coverage
- [SFF Chronicles forum thread: "Malice Box Quest" (started Jan 10, 2007 — active through Feb 13, 2007)](https://www.sffchronicles.com/threads/35047/) — Confirms Google Earth navigation, international players, leaderboard, Trial 5 timing, physical finale

### Campaign URL (Defunct)
- `maliceboxquest.com` — dead (domain no longer resolves)
- *Wayback Machine archives exist but individual snapshots not yet retrieved*

### Research Gaps
- Crackunit.com posts about this project not yet located (likely on page 2+ of January 2007 archive — Wayback has 592 captures of crackunit.com from this period)
- Unfiction forum thread not retrieved
- Trade press (Campaign, NMA, Revolution) coverage likely paywalled
- No video record found

nodes.title: Netted by the Webbys
nodes.desc: **David-Michel Davies**, Executive Director of The Webby Awards (MarketingSherpa, September 2011):
nodes.body:
# Netted by the Webbys

POKE London provided the naming and brand/design identity for **Netted** — a free daily email newsletter launched by The Webby Awards on 4 November 2009, dedicated to "better living through the internet." Each edition recommended one website, app, or service. The newsletter grew to 50,000+ subscribers and remains live at netted.net in 2026.

---

## POKE's Role

**David-Michel Davies**, Executive Director of The Webby Awards (MarketingSherpa, September 2011):

> *"I took our branding/design assignment to **Nicolas Roope and Iain Tait at Poke London** (Iain's now at Wieden+Kennedy) because I knew that when I asked them to make something both beautiful and Internet-y with a great name, they'd understand what that meant for our organization."*

This is the primary source confirming POKE's scope: **naming + branding/design**. The name "Netted by the Webbys" was itself the creative resolution — a name that connected the new property to the Webby Awards brand without causing confusion with the awards themselves.

**Note:** Iain had moved to W+K by the time Davies gave this quote (September 2011). The work was done while Iain was still at POKE (launched November 2009), at which point he was ECD.

---

## What Netted Was

A free daily email newsletter from The Webby Awards:
- One recommendation per issue — a website, app, or service
- Tagline: "Better living through the internet"
- Launched: 4 November 2009
- Grew to: 50,000+ subscribers
- Still live at `netted.net` in 2026 (now publishing periodic articles rather than daily emails)

**POKE–Webby relationship context:** Claire Graves, who later became Executive Director of The Webby Awards, had previously been a Client Lead at POKE London managing accounts including The Guardian, BBC, and Skype. The project reflects a deep, ongoing relationship between the two organisations.

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — Naming and branding lead, POKE London (explicitly named by client)

nodes.title: Orange Talking Point
nodes.desc: In 2005–2006, online public participation still meant:
nodes.body:
# Orange Talking Point

A debate and discussion microsite for Orange UK that pioneered an interface mechanic pre-dating the mainstream tag cloud and social media participation patterns. Users could engage with popular debate topics not by typing comments (the standard web forum model of the time) but by clicking on weighted, visual representations of topics and positions — a "click to add your voice" interaction that surfaced public consensus at a glance.

Won the **Webby Award in the Telecommunications category at the 10th Annual Webby Awards (May 2006)** — specifically cited for *"Pioneering social Media for Brand Engagement."* One of two Webby Awards POKE won in 2006 (alongside the the Jamie Oliver Feed Me Better campaign).

---

## The Innovation

In 2005–2006, online public participation still meant:
- Long-form forum posts (registration required)
- Amazon-style reviews (effortful)
- Blog comments (growing but still text-heavy)

Twitter did not yet exist. Facebook was US-college-only. Del.icio.us had pioneered tag clouds as a visualisation tool (c.2003–2004), but no one had turned the tag cloud into an *interaction mechanic* — a way to vote, participate, and make your voice felt by clicking.

Talking Point's innovation was exactly this: a popularity-weighted visual display of debate topics, where clicking registered participation, and the visual weight of each item reflected collective opinion. It predated what we now call upvoting, reaction buttons, and emoji responses by several years.

**Webby citation:** *"Won for 'Pioneering social Media for Brand Engagement'"* — framed at the time as an early form of social media, which is accurate. This was brand-hosted public discourse before any of the platforms we now associate with that concept existed.

---

## Awards

- **Webby Award, 10th Annual Webby Awards, May 2006** — Judges' Award, Telecommunications category (Websites / General Websites)
  - URL slug: `/2006/websites/general-websites/telecommunications/orange-talking-point`
  - Note: This is the standard Judges' Award, not the People's Voice (Jamie Oliver Feed Me Better won People's Voice in Activism the same year)

---

## POKE–Mother Relationship

POKE London operated as the "digital sister agency" to Mother, which held Orange's main advertising account. This institutional arrangement — confirmed in Campaign magazine's September 2006 digital survey — was the route through which POKE won the Talking Point brief. The relationship had been running for approximately five years by 2006. Campaign scored the POKE/Mother partnership 6/7 ("Excellent"), citing Talking Point as the primary evidence.

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Nik Roope](../collaborators/nik_roope.md)** — Co-Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Simon Waterfall](../collaborators/simon_waterfall.md)** — Co-founder / Creative Director, POKE London (lists Webby 2006 on CV)
- **Orange UK** — Client (via Mother agency relationship)
- **Mother** — Lead agency (held the Orange account; POKE was digital partner)
- *Individual designer/developer credits: not yet identified from open sources*

---

## References & Media

### Award and press
- [The Guardian, 9 May 2006 — "Guardian wins another Webby" (Mark Sweney) — confirms Poke's second Webby for Orange Talking Point](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2006/may/09/news.newmedia)
- [Campaign, 29 September 2006 — "Digital: All talk no digital" (Lucy Aitken) — cites Talking Point as creative highlight of Poke/Mother partnership](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk/article/digital-talk-no-digital/595913)
- [Simon Waterfall CV — lists "2006 / Webby award / Orange Talking point"](https://waterfall.co.uk/cv/talks-awards)

### Webby Awards
- [Webby entry URL (registration required to view)](https://winners.webbyawards.com/2006/websites/general-websites/telecommunications/orange-talking-point)

### Archived site
- Original URL: `http://www.orange.co.uk/talkingpoint/` (now offline)
- Wayback Machine search target: `https://web.archive.org/web/*/orange.co.uk*talkingpoint*`

nodes.title: Orange Unlimited — The Never-Ending Webpage
nodes.desc: **URL:** `http://unlimited.orange.co.uk` (also accessible as `http://www.goodthingsshouldneverend.co.uk`) — both now offline.
nodes.body:
# Orange Unlimited — The Never-Ending Webpage

**Official title:** *Unlimited — Good Things Should Never End*

A Flash-based infinite-scrolling webpage created by POKE London for Orange UK, launched in November 2007 as part of a £6m campaign to promote Orange's unlimited calls and texts packages. The concept was elegantly literal: a webpage that never ended — illustrating "unlimited" through the experience of scrolling forever, through animated characters, generative art, and reactive sound design.

Named in **Campaign Annual 2007 Top 10 Digital Ads (#8)** and **Advertising Age's best non-TV digital efforts of 2007**. Won a **Webby Award 2008 (Design Aesthetic)**, **Cannes Cyber Lions Silver**, **ADC New York Gold Cube**, **BIMA Overall Winner**, and the **Revolution Awards Grand Prix**.

**Attribution note:** Iain Tait contributed to the original concept as co-founder and co-creative director of POKE. Nik Roope led the craft, execution, and public presentation of the work. The Campaign Annual 2007 credits list Nik Roope first. Neither should be sole-credited — this was a shared origination.

---

## The Experience

**URL:** `http://unlimited.orange.co.uk` (also accessible as `http://www.goodthingsshouldneverend.co.uk`) — both now offline.

A Flash website designed to be genuinely endless. Users scrolled down — and down — encountering a continuous landscape of animated characters, illustrated vignettes, generative visual elements coded by Marius Watz, and a reactive audio layer by Nick Ryan. The page would not end because Orange's service did not end.

**Nik Roope (Dezeen, 2007):** *"Orange asked us to talk about their unlimited offers so we made them a never ending web page. Easy concept to sell but pretty scary when we had to work out what to put on it. We worked with an animator called Rex and an art coder guy called Mariuz Watz."*

**Campaign Annual 2007 description:** *"A website building on Orange's unlimited text package that runs for as long as you can be bothered to scroll down it. It's not really neverending, but is full of lovely illustrations and widgets."*

**Advertising Age (Dec 2007):** *"In a freedom-to-talk push, Poke, London created a never-ending webpage. Really, it doesn't stop."*

---

## Awards

| Award | Category | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Webby Awards 2008 | Design Aesthetic | **Winner** |
| Cannes Cyber Lions 2008 | Telecomms and Microsites | **Silver Lion** |
| Cannes Cyber Lions 2008 | Innovations | **Bronze Lion** |
| ADC New York | Innovation | **Gold Cube** |
| ADC New York | Best Microsite | **Silver Cube** |
| One Show Interactive | Consumer Microsites | **Silver Pencil** |
| One Show Interactive | Multimedia | **Bronze Pencil** |
| BIMA 2008 | Overall Winner — BTC Web | **Winner** |
| Revolution Awards | Grand Prix | **Winner** |
| Revolution Awards | Best Online Advertising | **Winner** |
| LIA 2008 | Best Use of Animation/Motion Graphics | Finalist |
| IAB | Campaign of the Year | **Winner** |
| Clio Awards | Best Microsite | **Bronze** |

*Note: Awards confirmed via Nicky Gibson's portfolio (nickygibson.com/exhibitions-awards). The Campaign Annual creatives list and Designboom/Cannes shortlist confirm the project's award trajectory. Individual award-level disambiguation against the full Poke credits list is noted.*

**Industry recognition:**
- **Campaign Annual 2007 Top 10 Digital Ads — #8** (same list as Spot the Bull at #1)
- **Advertising Age best non-TV digital 2007** — listed alongside Halo 3 and Discovery Channel Shark Runners

---

## Museum / Permanent Collection

Team member **Nicky Gibson** describes herself as "MoMA and Design Museum exhibited" on her personal website, with Orange Unlimited explicitly listed in her client work. She has a confirmed MoMA artist page at `moma.org/artists/39782` and exhibition credits at MoMA (New York), the Design Museum (London), and ICA (London).

Whether **Orange Unlimited specifically** is in MoMA's or the Design Museum's permanent collection or was exhibited as part of a digital design show requires manual verification:
- `https://www.moma.org/artists/39782` (Nicky Gibson MoMA page)
- `https://collection.designmuseum.org` (search "Poke" or "Orange Unlimited")

**Separate fact:** Nik Roope's **Plumen 001** lightbulb (his Hulger product, separate from POKE work) is confirmed in MoMA's and the V&A's permanent collections. This is not related to Orange Unlimited.

---

## Cultural Legacy

Orange Unlimited is one of three works cited as defining Iain Tait's POKE era in *Boards Magazine* (2010) — alongside Room 10101 and Oasis Rubberduckzilla. In the 2009 Creative Bloq profile of Iain Tait it is described simply as "the much-loved 'never-ending webpage' for Orange Unlimited" — no explanation required, indicating it had achieved genuine cultural recognition.

Nik Roope presented the work at **Belgrade Design Week 2009** and at the **Kyoorius Design Yatra seminar** (India, 2008) as an example of POKE's philosophy of ideas that exist as genuine experiences rather than advertisements.

Adage citations also note that Skittles' "Experience the Rainbow" campaign (2010) drew inspiration from this project.

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — Concept originator / Co-founder, POKE London (executive creative; not in Campaign credits)
- **[Nik Roope](../collaborators/nik_roope.md)** — Creative Lead / Co-founder, POKE London (listed first in Campaign credits; public face of the project)
- **[Nicky Gibson](../collaborators/nicky_gibson.md)** — Designer / Art Director (Campaign credits; MoMA + Design Museum exhibition credits)
- **[Derek McKenna](../collaborators/derek_mckenna.md)** — Lead Flash Developer (Campaign credits; also on Balloonacy)
- **[Rex Crowle](../collaborators/rex_crowle.md)** — Animator (named by Roope in Dezeen; rexbox.co.uk)
- **[Marius Watz](../collaborators/marius_watz.md)** — Art Coder / Generative (named by Roope in Dezeen; unlekker.net)
- **[Nick Ryan](../collaborators/nick_ryan.md)** — Sound / Audio (Campaign credits)
- **Julie Barnes** — Creative (Campaign credits)
- **Orange UK** — Client

---

## References & Media

### Assets

![poke_orange_unlimited img 02](../raw/media/poke_orange_unlimited/poke_orange_unlimited_img_02.gif)
![poke_orange_unlimited img 03](../raw/media/poke_orange_unlimited/poke_orange_unlimited_img_03.gif)
![poke_orange_unlimited img 04](../raw/media/poke_orange_unlimited/poke_orange_unlimited_img_04.jpg)
![poke_orange_unlimited img 05](../raw/media/poke_orange_unlimited/poke_orange_unlimited_img_05.jpg)

### Press

- [Core77, 3 November 2007 — "The never ending web page"](https://www.core77.com/posts/7988/The-never-ending-web-page)
- [Campaign Live, 1 November 2007 — "Orange launches colourful 'never-ending' campaign" (£6m campaign context)](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk/article/orange-launches-colourful-never-ending-campaign/764110)

- [Advertising Age, 17 December 2007 — named in best non-TV digital of 2007 (paywalled)](https://www.proquest.com/docview/208407336)

### Awards and collaborator pages

nodes.title: Room 10101 (Nokia / Designer's Block)
nodes.desc: 1. Visitors entered the room at Designer's Block, London
nodes.body:
# Room 10101

**Venue:** Designer's Block, London

A physical/digital installation created by POKE London for Nokia and exhibited at Designer's Block, London, 2004. Visitors sent an SMS to a dedicated Nokia number containing any word of their choice. The system performed a real-time Google Image Search on that word and projected the resulting images onto the walls of the room — turning the space into a dynamically shifting visual environment built entirely from visitors' collective imagination.

Named one of three defining works of Iain's POKE tenure in *Boards Magazine* (2010), alongside Oasis Rubberduckzilla and Orange Unlimited. Won **BIMA 2004, Best Arts and Culture**.

---

## The Experience

1. Visitors entered the room at Designer's Block, London
2. They texted any word to a dedicated Nokia SMS number
3. The system "hacked" Google Image Search — searching in real-time for that word
4. The returned images were projected onto the walls of the room
5. The room was continuously, dynamically repainted by visitors' text submissions

**The name:** "Room 10101" is a binary reference to George Orwell's *Room 101* from *Nineteen Eighty-Four* — the room where the Thought Police confronted each prisoner with their personal worst fear. The binary reframing (10101 = 21 in decimal) both elevates the joke mathematically and inverts the concept entirely: instead of a room containing fears, this was a room decorated by collective imagination and curiosity.

**Nokia's role:** The client provided both the brief and the mobile SMS infrastructure. In 2004, Nokia was the dominant global handset manufacturer, and this project positioned Nokia at the intersection of mobile communication and participatory cultural experience — well before smartphones.

---

## Historical Significance

Room 10101 sits at the intersection of several practices that were just forming in 2004:

**SMS as creative input (2004):** Using SMS to participate in a physical installation was genuinely novel. The iPhone was three years away; SMS was the primary mobile interaction mode.

**"Hacked" API as creative tool:** Google launched its Image Search in 2001 and its developer API in 2002. Using it as a generative, real-time artistic engine was an early example of what would later be called "API art" — software infrastructure co-opted for creative expression.

**Participatory projection installation:** Visitors literally built the room's visual environment in real time. This predates the mainstream projection-mapping and participatory installation wave of the late 2000s by several years.

**Proto-AR framing:** An algorithmically-generated image layer overlaid onto a physical space, triggered by mobile input. A decade before AR became a widespread concept.

Designer's Block as the exhibition venue specifically positioned this as cultural/arts work rather than advertising — confirmed by the BIMA Best Arts and Culture category win.

---

## Awards

- **BIMA 2004 — Best Arts and Culture** (source: Simon Waterfall's CV at waterfall.co.uk/cv/talks-awards — listed as "Room 101", confirmed as abbreviation of Room 10101)

**Other POKE BIMA 2004 wins in the same submission cycle:** Best Integrated Campaign; Best Online Relationship: Marketing Orange; Best Banking and Finance: Yahoo.

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Nik Roope](../collaborators/nik_roope.md)** — Co-Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Simon Waterfall](../collaborators/simon_waterfall.md)** — Co-founder / Creative Director, POKE London (BIMA win appears on his CV)
- **[Nick Farnhill](../collaborators/nick_farnhill.md)** — CEO / co-founder, POKE London
- **Nokia** — Client and SMS technology platform
- *Developer/technical credits: not yet identified from open sources*

---

## References & Media

### Primary sources
- [W+K London / Boards Magazine, March 2010 — "Iain Tait on joining Wieden+Kennedy" (Nokia / Room 10101 cited as one of three defining POKE works)](https://wklondon.com/2010/03/iain-tait-on-joining-wieden-kennedy/)
- [Simon Waterfall CV — BIMA 2004 Best Arts and Culture listing](https://waterfall.co.uk/cv/talks-awards)

### Research gaps
- No video documentation found (predates YouTube by one year)
- No photographs in public archives
- No contemporary trade press coverage retrieved (NMA, Revolution, Design Week archives not indexed online for 2004)
- Designer's Block 2004 programme not located

nodes.title: Nick Farnhill
nodes.body:
# Nick Farnhill

Nick Farnhill co-founded POKE London in 2001 as one of six founders (Iain Tait, Nik Roope, Simon Waterfall, Peter Beech, Tom Hostler) and served as Chief Executive throughout the agency's independent years. He remained CEO through the Publicis Groupe acquisition in September 2013, continuing to run Publicis.Poke until later co-founding **FOOD** with Iain Tait and Richard Turley in 2021. *(evidence: raw/archive/food_creative_studio.md; raw/research/poke_london_agency_2026-04-06.md)*

## Worked With Iain On

- **[The Warholiser](../projects/poke_warholiser.md)** — CEO / co-founder, POKE London (2002). *(evidence: projects/poke_warholiser.md)*
- **[Room 10101](../projects/poke_room_10101.md)** — CEO / co-founder, POKE London (2004). *(evidence: projects/poke_room_10101.md)*
- **FOOD** — Co-founder, 2021–present. *(evidence: raw/archive/food_creative_studio.md)*

## References

nodes.title: Oasis Rubberduckzilla — AR Campaign
nodes.desc: ### AR Games — rubberduckzilla.com
nodes.body:
# Oasis Rubberduckzilla — AR Campaign

POKE London's digital and augmented reality work in support of the Oasis "Rubberduckzilla" campaign — a giant, water-hating rubber duck stomping through a Japanese city in the style of Godzilla. **The TV ad, print, and outdoor were made by Mother London (not POKE).** POKE created the AR games, the interactive website, and the innovative Sun newspaper media takeover.

One of the earliest examples of augmented reality used in mainstream UK brand advertising — deployed in 2009, using FLARToolKit when AR was still genuinely novel.

**Iain (crackunit.com, June 2009):** *"The Oasis stuff feels like a slightly silly way of delivering what is essentially a slightly silly message. It's not trying to do anything more. Which is cool in my book."*

---

## What POKE Built

### AR Games — rubberduckzilla.com

A Flash-based AR games website using **FLARToolKit** (open-source AR library). At launch two of four planned sections were active:

1. **Become Rubberduckzilla** — users held an AR marker up to their webcam to become RDZ and fire lasers from their eyes
2. **Head Takeover** — putting the AR marker on your head turned your head into the duck's head

Interaction was playful and low-stakes — matching the brand's tone exactly. 8-bit Japanese-style graphics and music.

### The Sun Newspaper Takeover

The standout component. POKE created a special "episode" in partnership with The Sun: **the physical front cover of The Sun newspaper became an AR marker**. Holding a copy of The Sun up to a webcam replaced the cover with animated destruction content.

Iain, crackunit.com: *"Personally I like this bit of the campaign as it puts the 'marker' in peoples' hands and doesn't rely on printers and stuff."*

This was genuinely innovative — solving the primary friction of consumer AR (the need to print markers) by turning a mass-circulation newspaper into the input device. At the time, The Sun sold approximately 3 million copies daily.

---

## Agency Split

| Work | Agency |
|---|---|
| TV ad (directed by Joseph Kahn) | Mother London + HSI Productions |
| Digital / AR / interactive | **POKE London** |

The TV campaign launched 18 May 2009 (first aired during Hollyoaks). POKE's AR site launched approximately June 2009. The two agencies worked in parallel — POKE and Mother had a long-standing partnership (Mother held many brand accounts, with POKE as their digital arm).

---

## Technology

- **FLARToolKit** — Flash-based open-source AR library (community-built from ARToolKit)
- **Webcam + printed AR markers** — standard 2009 AR stack
- **The Sun as AR marker** — the campaign's key innovation; eliminated printer dependency

**Context:** In 2009, AR was barely emerging in consumer applications. The iPhone 3GS (the first with a compass, enabling location AR) had just launched. Markerless AR was years away. FLARToolKit marker-based AR was the cutting edge. Flo Heiss (then at Dare) said at the time: *"Augmented Reality is the Quicktime VR of 2009"* — positioning it as a hyped-but-embryonic technology.

---

## Awards

No confirmed award wins or nominations found for POKE's digital component. Research gap: BIMA or Cannes Cyber Lions records from 2009–2010 may yield results.

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — ECD, POKE London
- **[Gavin Fox](../collaborators/gavin_fox.md)** — Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Katie Marcus](../collaborators/katie_marcus.md)** — Creative, POKE London
- **[Jason Fox](../collaborators/jason_fox.md)** — Creative, POKE London
- **Mother London** — TV, print, and outdoor (NOT POKE's work)
- **Joseph Kahn** — TV director (Mother / HSI)
- **Oasis / Coca-Cola UK** — Client (client contact: Cathryn Sleight)

---

## References & Media

### Assets
![poke_rubberduckzilla animated](../raw/media/poke_rubberduckzilla/2009_poke_rubberduckzilla.gif)

![poke_rubberduckzilla frame 01](../raw/media/poke_rubberduckzilla/2009_poke_rubberduckzilla_frame_01.jpg)
![poke_rubberduckzilla frame 02](../raw/media/poke_rubberduckzilla/2009_poke_rubberduckzilla_frame_02.jpg)
![poke_rubberduckzilla frame 03](../raw/media/poke_rubberduckzilla/2009_poke_rubberduckzilla_frame_03.jpg)
![poke_rubberduckzilla frame 04](../raw/media/poke_rubberduckzilla/2009_poke_rubberduckzilla_frame_04.jpg)
![poke_rubberduckzilla frame 05](../raw/media/poke_rubberduckzilla/2009_poke_rubberduckzilla_frame_05.jpg)

# Gavin Fox

## Overview

Gavin Fox was a Creative Director at POKE London, confirmed there from 2009 through at least 2013. He was one of the lead creatives on the Oasis Rubberduckzilla AR campaign (2009) working under ECD Iain Tait.

After POKE, he joined Framestore's immersive department in 2014, going on to win major industry awards for theme park/immersive experience work: the Lionsgate Entertainment World "Midnight Ride" (Thea Award, 2021), Jurassic World Adventure (VES Award, 2022), and Gardaland Jumanji The Adventure (VES nominated, 2023).

nodes.title: Orange Spot the Bull
nodes.desc: **The bull:** A real animal fitted with a GPS tracker, placed in a secret field at an undisclosed UK location. Not at Glastonbury — the Glastonbury connection was entirely the prize.
nodes.body:
# Orange Spot the Bull

**Years:** 2007, 2008, 2009 (three consecutive annual campaigns)

A three-year annual digital campaign for Orange UK, tied to Orange's long-running Glastonbury Festival sponsorship. A real bull was fitted with a GPS tracker and placed in a real field at a secret UK location (not at Glastonbury). Users visited a dedicated microsite, viewed the bull's live position via GPS data, and picked the grid square where they predicted the bull would be standing at 3pm that day. Correct guessers entered a daily prize draw to win a pair of Glastonbury Festival tickets.

Named **#1 in Campaign Annual 2007's Top 10 Digital Ads**. Won **two LIA Gold awards in 2008**. Named one of the "10 Best Ever Promotions" in the UK by Mando Agency in 2025, and cited as the direct inspiration for Walkers' "When it Rains" promotional campaign.

---

## The Experience

**The bull:** A real animal fitted with a GPS tracker, placed in a secret field at an undisclosed UK location. Not at Glastonbury — the Glastonbury connection was entirely the prize.

**The mechanic:**
1. Users visited `spotthebull.co.uk` (also `spotthebull.orange.co.uk`)
2. The bull's live position was displayed on a grid map of the field
3. Users picked the grid square they predicted the bull would occupy at 3pm that day
4. Correct predictions entered a daily prize draw for a pair of Glastonbury Festival tickets

**The prize:** Sold-out Glastonbury Festival tickets — effectively priceless to UK music fans, highly contextually relevant to Orange's Glastonbury sponsorship.

### Campaign Evolution by Year

**2007 — Original launch**
Named #1 in Campaign Annual's Top 10 Digital Ads of 2007. TrendHunter covered it in May 2007: *"A brilliantly funny idea that really took off."* The bull in some early coverage is referred to as "Derek."

**2008 — Enhanced relaunch**
Added hoof-step-by-hoof-step tracking. A farmer provided daily tips on the bull's routine. Campaign Live reported the relaunch on 30 May 2008: "Orange revives Spot the Bull." Technology stack expanded to include Papervision3D, Flash AS3, and a live two-way GPS data feed via Flashtalking's 3D banner format. Production partnership with Unit 9 confirmed.

**2009 — Third iteration**
Bull named **Desmond**. Added Twitter account @spotthebull. AdPulp: *"Ride Darling Desmond To The Show"* (27 May 2009). Social media integration formalised.

---

## Awards

- **Campaign Annual 2007: #1 in Top 10 Digital Ads** — "A brilliantly funny idea that really took off. Orange had some Glastonbury tickets to give away, so Poke put a GPS tracker on a bull…"
- **LIA 2008: Gold — Digital Media / Online Games** (POKE London / Orange / "Spot The Bull")
- **LIA 2008: Gold — Digital Media / Best Use of Interactivity** (POKE London / Orange / "Spot The Bull")

---

## Cultural Legacy

In 2025, Mando Agency named Spot the Bull one of **"The 10 Best Ever Promotions"** in UK advertising, explicitly citing it as the direct creative inspiration for Walkers' "When it Rains" campaign:

> *"It was this promotion which inspired Walkers' When it Rains promotion which was a much bigger promotional campaign involving consumers purchasing crisps and picking grid squares across the country to participate — by guessing where it was going to rain."*

Spot the Bull was one of the earliest documented examples of **physical-digital real-time integration in UK advertising** — a real object connected to the internet to drive digital engagement, predating mainstream IoT discourse by nearly a decade. The campaign ran at the exact moment that Flash-based 3D banners (Papervision3D) were pushing browser-based interactivity to its limits.

When POKE celebrated their 10th anniversary in 2011, Spot the Bull was one of the works commemorated with a bespoke cake display — confirming its status as one of the agency's landmark projects.

---

## Technology

- **Papervision3D** (Flash AS3) — 3D rendering in the browser
- **Live two-way GPS data feed** — bull's position relayed in real-time
- **Flashtalking** — 3D interactive banner ad format (showcase at flashtalking.com/showcase/Orange3D/)
- **Twitter** — 2009 iteration, @spotthebull

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Nik Roope](../collaborators/nik_roope.md)** — Co-Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Oliver Wright](../collaborators/oliver_wright.md)** — Executive Producer, POKE London
- **[Nilesh Ashra](../collaborators/nilesh_ashra.md)** — Developer, POKE London
- **[Unit 9](../collaborators/unit9.md)** — Production partner (2008–2009)
- **Mediaedge CIA** — Media planning and buying
- **Flashtalking** — Ad technology platform (3D banner unit)
- **Orange UK** — Client

---

## References & Media

### Assets
![poke_spot_the_bull img 01](../raw/media/poke_spot_the_bull/poke_spot_the_bull_img_01.png)
![poke_spot_the_bull img 02](../raw/media/poke_spot_the_bull/poke_spot_the_bull_img_02.jpg)

### Awards database
- [LIA 2008 winners — Digital Media (Online Games + Best Use of Interactivity)](https://2008.liaentries.com/winners/?id_medium=2&view=icons&range=w)

### Press
- [Campaign Annual 2007: Top 10 Digital Ads (#1)](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk/article/campaign-annual-2007-top-10-digital-ads/773787)
- [Campaign Live, 27 May 2008: "Orange launches Glastonbury ticket giveaway online"](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk/article/orange-launches-glastonbury-ticket-giveaway-online/811722)
- [Campaign Live, 30 May 2008: "Orange revives Spot the Bull"](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk/article/week-advertising-news-orange-revives-spot-bull/812871)
- [Campaign Live, 23 May 2008: "Orange celebrates 10 years at Glasto with 'kinetic' charge"](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk/article/orange-celebrates-10-years-glasto-kinetic-charge/811650)
- [Campaign Live, 25 Nov 2008: "Brand Watch: Case Study — Orange"](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk/article/brand-watch-case-study-orange/864869)
- [TrendHunter, 31 May 2007: "Spot the Bull Ad Campaign"](https://www.trendhunter.com/trends/viral-marketing-spot-the-bull-win-glastonbury-music-festival-tickets)
- [AdPulp, 27 May 2009: "Ride Darling Desmond To The Show"](https://www.adpulp.com/ride_darling_de/)
- [Mando Agency, 9 July 2025: "The 10 Best Ever Promotions" (#4 — Spot the Bull)](https://mando.co.uk/10-best-ever-promotions/)
- [If Only We'd Thought of That blog, 16 June 2009](https://ifonlyblog.wordpress.com/2009/06/16/orange-spot-the-bull-digital-campaign/)
- [Cakeheadloves, December 2011 — POKE 10th birthday (Spot the Bull among landmark works)](https://cakeheadloves.wordpress.com/our-work/poke-10/)

### Archived site
- Microsite: `http://www.spotthebull.co.uk/` (now offline)

nodes.title: Very Public Art Exhibition
nodes.desc: Selfridges' Oxford Street windows — some of the most-viewed retail display space in London — became an art gallery. Each work was displayed with an SMS shortcode; passers-by could bid simply by texting from the street.
nodes.body:
# Very Public Art Exhibition

A physical art exhibition installed in Selfridges' famous Oxford Street shop windows, where members of the public could bid on British art via SMS — standing on the pavement, looking through the glass. Designed by POKE London in 2003, years before mobile commerce or m-ticketing were mainstream concepts.

Won **BIMA 2003** and **One Show New York Gold 2004**.

---

## The Experience

Selfridges' Oxford Street windows — some of the most-viewed retail display space in London — became an art gallery. Each work was displayed with an SMS shortcode; passers-by could bid simply by texting from the street.

The name captures the concept precisely: art that was literally, physically very public — visible to everyone on Oxford Street, with participation requiring only the phone in your pocket.

**Why this was ahead of its time (2003):** Mobile commerce barely existed. SMS was the dominant mobile interaction. Using it as a real-time bidding and transaction mechanism for art — in a street-facing context — anticipated both m-commerce and the participatory retail experiences that would become commonplace a decade later.

---

## Awards

- **BIMA 2003** — Win (source: Simon Waterfall CV, waterfall.co.uk/cv/talks-awards — listed as *"Very public art exhibition" Selfridges shop window auction with SMS for Brit Art*)
- **One Show New York — Gold, 2004** — Same project, US submission cycle (source: Simon Waterfall CV)

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — ECD, POKE London
- **[Nik Roope](../collaborators/nik_roope.md)** — Co-Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Simon Waterfall](../collaborators/simon_waterfall.md)** — Co-founder / Creative Director, POKE London (project listed on his CV)
- **Selfridges** — Client / Venue
- *Individual creative/developer credits: not yet identified from open sources*

---

## References & Media

- [Simon Waterfall CV — BIMA 2003 + One Show Gold 2004 confirmation](https://waterfall.co.uk/cv/talks-awards)
- [Design Week, 3 April 2003 — "Auction" (paywalled; snippet confirms "creative elements designed by Poke. Venue: Selfridges, 400 Oxford Street, London W1")](https://www.designweek.co.uk/issues/3-april-2003/auction-3/)

nodes.title: The Warholiser
nodes.desc: 1. Users visited the Tate website during the exhibition period (February–April 2002)
nodes.body:
# The Warholiser

One of POKE London's first significant digital commissions — a user-participation website built for Tate Modern's *Warhol* retrospective exhibition (7 February – 1 April 2002). Users uploaded a photo of themselves; POKE's software applied a Warhol-style pop art treatment; the resulting portrait was displayed live on the Tate website for exactly 15 minutes.

The 15-minute mechanic was a direct conceptual reference to Andy Warhol's most famous (if mythologised) statement: "In the future, everyone will be world-famous for 15 minutes." The work predated social media photo sharing and user-generated content culture by several years.

---

## The Experience

1. Users visited the Tate website during the exhibition period (February–April 2002)
2. They uploaded a photo of themselves
3. POKE's software applied a Warhol-style colour separation and pop art treatment
4. The resulting portrait appeared live on the Tate site for 15 minutes — a digital "15 minutes of fame"

**Scale (from Nik Roope, Shots Magazine, 2016):** "Thousands uploaded images and hundreds of thousands visited the site."

**Nik Roope's framing (Shots, 2016):** *"It wasn't an ad. It was Warhol's art as a completely scaleable experience that you could participate in, or enjoy as some nice images to look at... That was amazing, and rammed home that there was this new space opening up, an appetite for a new kind of proposition and experience."*

The work was an early proof-of-concept for participatory digital experience — user-generated content, ephemeral display, and direct audience participation with an artist's legacy — ideas that would not become mainstream for another five or six years.

---

## Exhibition Context

**Exhibition:** *Warhol* at Tate Modern, London
**Dates:** 7 February – 1 April 2002
**Sponsor:** UBS Warburg
**Curator:** Donna De Salvo
**Scale:** Over 200 works — described at the time as the largest Warhol retrospective ever staged

The exhibition was a major London cultural event: Channel 4 broadcast a documentary series, and Warhol imagery appeared on billboards across the city. The Warholiser was the digital extension of this campaign.

---

## Awards

- **BIMA 2002** — "The Tate Modern 'Warholiser' website for exhibitions" (source: Simon Waterfall's CV, `waterfall.co.uk/cv/talks-awards`). Category: website for exhibitions (Arts & Culture equivalent).

**Cannes shortlist:** Not confirmed or denied. Cannes 2002 archives are not fully indexed online. A shortlisting is plausible given POKE's subsequent awards trajectory, but cannot be stated as fact.

---

## Cultural Legacy

The Warholiser is cited in Faris Yakob's *Paid Attention: Innovative Advertising for a Digital World* (Kogan Page, 2015 and 2021 editions) as an early case study in innovative participatory digital advertising. Yakob is a respected industry strategist; the book is used in advertising education. This constitutes canonical industry-textbook status.

In 2016, Nik Roope described the Warholiser as the foundational experience that established POKE's "living ideas" philosophy — the idea that digital work could be an experience rather than an advertisement.

---

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/)** — Creative Director, POKE London
- **[Nik Roope](../collaborators/nik_roope.md)** — Co-Creative Director, POKE London (discusses the project as a co-founder success in Shots, 2016)
- **[Simon Waterfall](../collaborators/simon_waterfall.md)** — Co-founder / Creative Director, POKE London (lists BIMA 2002 award on personal CV)
- **[Nick Farnhill](../collaborators/nick_farnhill.md)** — CEO / co-founder, POKE London
- **[Naked Communications](../collaborators/naked_communications.md)** — Partner agency
- **Tate Modern** — Client (commissioned as part of the *Warhol* exhibition programme)
- *Developer/Designer credits: not yet identified from open sources*

---

## References & Media

### Live pages
- [Tate Modern: Warhol exhibition page (official, still live)](https://www.tate.org.uk/whats-on/tate-modern/warhol)
- [Tate press release, 5 February 2002](https://www.tate.org.uk/press/press-releases/warhol)

### Award source
- [Simon Waterfall CV — BIMA 2002 confirmation](https://waterfall.co.uk/cv/talks-awards)

### Contemporary documentation (2002)
- [Flashback News Agency #9, Sweden, 13 February 2002 — lists live URL tate.cix.co.uk with "Warholise a picture" description](https://www.flashback.se/fna/surftips-9) — earliest confirmed reference to the Warholiser during the exhibition
- [The Guardian, 15 January 2002 — "Exhibition gives Warhol more than 15 minutes of fame"](https://www.theguardian.com/uk/2002/jan/15/arts.warhol)
- [Studio International, 12 February 2002 — exhibition review](https://www.studiointernational.com/andy-warhol)
- [London SE1 preview, 6 February 2002](https://www.london-se1.co.uk/news/view/142)

### Industry coverage
- [Shots Magazine — "Tech Special: Nik Roope" (c.2016) — key quote on the Warholiser as POKE's founding philosophy](https://shots.net/news/view/91076-tech-special-nik-roope)
- [Creative Bloq — "Iain Tait" profile (2009) — POKE founding context](https://www.creativebloq.com/3d/iain-tait-7099043)
- [Advertising Week Europe 2016 — confirms Iain Tait / Nik Roope as POKE co-founders](https://archive.advertisingweek.com/events/eu/2016/calendar/-europe-2016-04-20-the-poke-brilliant-lectures)

### Book citation
- [Faris Yakob, *Paid Attention* (2015, Scribd)](https://www.scribd.com/document/544596017/Faris-Yakob-Paid-Attention-Innovative-Advertising-for-a-Digital-World-Kogan-Page-2015)
- [Faris Yakob, *Paid Attention* 2nd edition (2021, Scribd)](https://www.scribd.com/document/569919329/Paid-attention-innovative-advertising-for-a-digital-world-2021)

### Archived URL
- Original live URL (February–April 2002): `http://tate.cix.co.uk/` (no longer active)
- Wayback Machine calendar (not fully archived): `https://web.archive.org/web/*/tate.org.uk/warhol*`

nodes.title: Naked Communications

# Naked Communications

Communications planning and media strategy agency founded in 2000 in London by John Harlow, Jon Wilkins and Will Collin (all former executives at Omnicom's PHD). Known for channel-neutral, unconventional strategy, Naked was acquired by Australia's Photon Group in 2008. They were a partner on POKE London's Warholiser project for Tate Modern (2002).

## Worked With Iain On

nodes.title: Bud Light UK Launch + Tyrrells: Absurdly Good
nodes.desc: See sub-campaign sections below for full collaborator details.
nodes.body:
# Bud Light UK Launch + Tyrrells: Absurdly Good

Two major UK brand establishment campaigns run in the same year.

---

## Collaborators

See sub-campaign sections below for full collaborator details.

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/iain_tait.md)** — Executive Creative Director, W+K London (both campaigns)
- **Tony Davidson** — Executive Creative Director, W+K London (both campaigns)
- **[James Guy](../collaborators/james_guy.md)** — Executive Producer / Head of Integrated Production, W+K London

---

## Bud Light: "Keep It Bud Light" (2017)

**First UK campaign for the brand.**

### Phase 1 — OOH Launch (March 2017)
National billboard rollout including the London IMAX and Birmingham Media Eyes. Tagline: **"Keep It Bud Light"**. Established the brand's visual identity across UK outdoor.

### Phase 2 — TV (October 2017)
A parody lifestyle ad — Bud Light playing with the conventions of premium beer advertising. Directed by **Ali Ali** through **Sonny London** — his first UK ad. (Ali Ali is the acclaimed Egyptian commercial director.)

### Collaborators — Bud Light
- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/iain_tait.md)** — Executive Creative Director
- **Tony Davidson** — Executive Creative Director
- **[Ray Shaughnessy](../collaborators/ray_shaughnessy.md)** — Creative Director
- **[Dan Norris](../collaborators/dan_norris.md)** — Creative Director
- **Ali Ali** — Director (Sonny London)

---

## Tyrrells: "Absurdly Good" (September 2017)

**First-ever television campaign for the brand.** Budget: £2.5m.

### The Work
Two spots launched 11 September 2017, running through to end of November:
- **"Very Important Potatoes"** — 20" TVC
- **"Fabulously Curly"** — 20" social film

Voiceover by **Peter Serafinowicz**. Maintained Tyrrells' distinctly eccentric, vintage aesthetic while scaling it to national television.

### Collaborators — Tyrrells
- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/iain_tait.md)** — Executive Creative Director
- **Tony Davidson** — Executive Creative Director
- **[Dan Norris](../collaborators/dan_norris.md)** — Creative Director
- **[Ray Shaughnessy](../collaborators/ray_shaughnessy.md)** — Creative Director
- **[Adam Newby](../collaborators/adam_newby.md)** — Creative
- **[Will Wells](../collaborators/will_wells.md)** — Creative
- **Greg Kouts** — Creative
- **Dog & Rabbit** — Production company / Directors
- **Peter Serafinowicz** — Voiceover

---

## References & Media

- [Campaign Live: Bud Light UK launch coverage](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk)
- [Ads of the World: Tyrrells "Absurdly Good"](https://www.adsoftheworld.com)
- [Raw research file](../raw/research/wk_london_brands_2026-04-07.md)

nodes.title: Wieden+Kennedy London
nodes.body:
# Wieden+Kennedy London

## Agency Overview

Wieden+Kennedy is a preeminent global independent creative agency network. The London office has produced some of the world's most culturally penetrative and highly-awarded work for clients like Nike, Honda, and Formula One.

## Iain Tait's Role

Iain Tait served as Executive Creative Director at Wieden+Kennedy London (and previously held digital leadership roles at the agency), where he helped steer the agency's creative output across a massive roster of global brands. A hallmark of his tenure was embracing digital, physical, and gaming intersectionality.

## Notable Synthesized Projects

* **[Wearing Gillian](../projects/wk_gillian_wearing_deepfake.md)**  
    *A collaborative film piece using early, advanced deepfake AI to permanently map the artist's face onto actors.*
* **[Nothing Beats a Londoner](../projects/wk_nothing_beats_a_londoner.md)**  
    *The multi-award-winning, hyper-local Nike campaign that achieved global resonance through intense cultural authenticity.*
* **[Formula 1 Global Rebrand](../projects/wk_f1_global_rebrand.md)**  
    *The highly polarizing, massive visual and strategic overhaul of F1, including the "Engineered Insanity" launch film.*
* **[Helping Everyone Eat Better](../projects/wk_helping_everyone_eat_better.md)**  
    *The cornerstone 2021 repositioning campaign for Sainsbury's, shifting them from pure value to health and environmental sustainability.*
* [Pokémon GO "City Celebrations"](../projects/wk_pokemon_go_city_celebrations.md)
* [Harry Potter: Wizards Unite](../projects/wk_harry_potter_wizards_unite.md)

## Source Files

* `raw/wk_niantic_campaigns.md`

nodes.title: Ray Shaughnessy
nodes.body:
# Ray Shaughnessy

Creative director who spent roughly thirteen years at Wieden+Kennedy London (joined 2006) as one half of a long-standing creative team with Dan Norris, working on accounts including Three, Formula 1, Lurpak, Sainsbury's, Tesco, Bud Light and Fairtrade. Shaughnessy and Norris left W+K London to join McCann London as Executive Creative Directors in 2020, and subsequently exited McCann. *(sources: Campaign, LBBonline, Adweek)*

## Worked With Iain On

nodes.body:
# Tony Davidson

Executive Creative Director at Wieden+Kennedy London for roughly two decades. Joined W+K in 2000 with then-creative partner Kim Papworth; became a global partner in 2009. After Papworth's departure in 2014, Iain Tait joined as his fellow ECD in London, and the pair led the London office together until Davidson stepped down in 2021. *(Source: Campaign, Creative Review career journey, W+K London partners announcement.)* In 2016 the British Arrows gave Davidson the Chairman's Award for his contribution to advertising. *(Source: More About Advertising.)*

nodes.title: Coca-Cola / Maroon 5: 24hr Recording Session
nodes.desc: Part of Coca-Cola's "Coca-Cola Music" initiative for teens. On **22 March 2011 at 17:00 GMT**, Maroon 5 entered a London studio and wrote and recorded an entirely original track from scratch, live in front of a global internet audience, over 24 continuous hours.
nodes.body:
# Coca-Cola / Maroon 5: 24hr Recording Session

## The Event

Part of Coca-Cola's "Coca-Cola Music" initiative for teens. On **22 March 2011 at 17:00 GMT**, Maroon 5 entered a London studio and wrote and recorded an entirely original track from scratch, live in front of a global internet audience, over 24 continuous hours.

## Interactive Execution

A large **projection-mapped interactive sculpture** inside the recording studio displayed fan submissions — lyrics, beats, comments — in real time via Twitter and social media. Band members could interact with the projections via gestures. The system was built by **Hellicar & Lewis** (Pete Hellicar & Joel Lewis) using **openFrameworks**, with a team of creative technologists.

Fans globally could influence the creative process as it happened — their contributions visibly shaping the physical environment the band was working in.

## The Song

**"Is Anybody Out There"** featuring **PJ Morton**. Available as a free download; for the first 100,000 downloads, Coca-Cola donated to the **RAIN** (Rural Action in Nigeria) clean water initiative for Africa.

- [YouTube: "Is Anybody Out There" — Maroon 5 ft. PJ Morton](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IJdWn96ldOE)

## Reach

| Metric | Figure |
|---|---|
| Countries reached (live stream) | 139 |
| Coca-Cola Facebook promotion reach | 20+ million |

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/iain_tait.md)** — Global Interactive Creative Director, W+K (authored the Campaign magazine first-person piece about the project; quoted in The Drum)
- **[Dom Felton](../collaborators/dom_felton.md)** — Producer, W+K London
- **[Nexus Studios](../collaborators/nexus_studios.md)** (then Nexus Interactive Arts) — Production / technology company
- **Hellicar & Lewis** (Pete Hellicar + **[Joel Gethin Lewis](../collaborators/joel_gethin_lewis.md)**) — Directing duo (on Nexus Interactive Arts roster); built gestural openFrameworks system
- **Chris Sugrue, James George, Josh Nimoy, Joshua Noble, Marek Bereza, Todd Vanderlin** — Hellicar & Lewis collaborators
- **PJ Morton** — Featured artist on the track
- **[Jackie Jantos](../collaborators/jackie_jantos.md)** — Client, Coca-Cola (per Iain Tait)
- **Joe Belliotti** — Director of Global Entertainment Marketing, Coca-Cola (named in press)

*No additional individual W+K writer or art director names confirmed in press sources.*

## References & Media

nodes.title: Wieden+Kennedy Portland
nodes.body:
# Wieden+Kennedy Portland

## Agency Overview

The Portland headquarters of Wieden+Kennedy is iconic in the advertising industry, historically renowned for its broadcast output and defining the voice of Nike. Between 2010 and 2012, the agency underwent a significant digital transformation.

## Iain Tait's Role

Iain Tait joined Wieden+Kennedy Portland as Global Interactive Executive Creative Director. His mandate was to elevate the agency's digital thinking to match its broadcast pedigree. Under his leadership, the interactive work achieved global cultural penetration, culminating in the first Digital Grand Prix for the agency.

## Notable Synthesized Projects

- [Old Spice "Responses"](../projects/wk_old_spice_responses.md)
- [Nike Better World](../projects/wk_nike_better_world.md)
- [Dodge Tent Event (Chimp Removal)](../projects/wk_dodge_tent_event.md)

## Source Files

- `raw/old_spice_responses.md`
- `raw/wk_portland_nike_dodge.md`

# Joel Gethin Lewis

Interaction designer and creative technologist. Co-founded **Hellicar & Lewis** (2008–2016) with Pete Hellicar, an open-source craft/design/technology studio, and was a co-founder of **YesYesNo** (2008) with Theo Watson and Zach Lieberman. Previously interaction designer at United Visual Artists (touring with U2 and Massive Attack). Currently Interactive Creative Director at Universal Everything and Course Leader of the UAL Diploma in Apple Development at the Creative Computing Institute, London. *(sources: [joelgethinlewis.com](https://joelgethinlewis.com/), [CreativeApplications.Net](https://www.creativeapplications.net/people/joel-gethin-lewis/), [Universal Everything](https://www.universaleverything.com/about-us/joel-gethin-lewis))*

nodes.title: Coke Polar Bowl
nodes.desc: Two animated Coca-Cola polar bears — one a New York Giants fan, one a New England Patriots fan — sat on their "snowfa" in an Arctic cave and watched Super Bowl XLVI live, in real-time, at **CokePolarBowl.com**.
nodes.body:
# Coke Polar Bowl

> *Referenced in "The Idea Writers" (Palgrave Macmillan) and a 2024 Ad Age retrospective on campaigns that "hacked" the Super Bowl.*

## The Concept

Two animated Coca-Cola polar bears — one a New York Giants fan, one a New England Patriots fan — sat on their "snowfa" in an Arctic cave and watched Super Bowl XLVI live, in real-time, at **CokePolarBowl.com**.

The bears reacted to every play, the Madonna halftime show, and every commercial — including rival ads (they'd fall asleep during dull ones). The audience watching online watched *with* the bears, turning the broadcast into a shared, animated second-screen experience.

Super Bowl XLVI drew **111.3 million TV viewers** — a Nielsen record at the time.

## Technical Execution

This was not real-time CGI rendering — it was a sophisticated **live puppeteering system**. From a **control room at MLB Advanced Media in New York**, animators drove the bears using **Xbox game controllers** and a proprietary PC-based triggering interface, assembling pre-rendered animation libraries in real-time to match the game's unfolding events.

**Framestore** (New York and London) built the CGI bears and the real-time puppeting technology. **Animal Logic** (Sydney) handled the broadcast TV spot animation. **MLB Advanced Media** provided the streaming infrastructure.

Copywriter Heather Ryder confirmed: *"my partner and I digitally 'puppeted' the Polar Bears on game day."*

## Awards

| Award | Category | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Cannes Lions 2012 | **Gold Direct Lion** — FMCG | **WIN** |
| Cannes Lions 2012 | **Silver Media Lion** — Best Use of Screens | **WIN** |
| Cannes Lions 2012 | Bronze Direct — Social Media | WIN |
| Cannes Lions 2012 | Bronze Film — Interactive Film | WIN |
| Cannes Lions 2012 | Bronze Branded Content | WIN |
| ANDY Awards | **Silver** — Integrated | WIN |
| ANDY Awards | **Silver** — Reset | WIN |
| Internationalist Awards | **Grand Prix** — Digital Marketing Innovation | WIN |
| WARC Creative 100 | Listed | — |

## Metrics

| Metric | Figure |
|---|---|
| Total digital streams | 9 million |
| Average viewing time | 28 minutes |
| Industry benchmark (avg viewing) | ~10 minutes |
| Coke social share-of-voice (game day) | #3 brand overall |

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/iain_tait.md)** — Global Interactive Executive Creative Director, W+K Portland
- **[Mark Fitzloff](../collaborators/mark_fitzloff.md)** — Executive Creative Director, W+K Portland
- **[Susan Hoffman](../collaborators/susan_hoffman.md)** — Executive Creative Director, W+K Portland
- **Hal Curtis** — Creative Director
- **Jeff Gillette** — Creative Director
- **Heather Ryder** — Copywriter (puppeted the bears live on game day)
- **Chris Thurman** — Art Director
- **Ben Grylewicz** — Executive Producer, W+K Portland
- **[Igor Clark](../collaborators/igor_clark.md)** — Creative Technology Director, W+K Portland *(evidence: campaignlive.co.uk)*
- **[Nilesh Ashra](../collaborators/nilesh_ashra.md)** — Creative Technology, W+K Portland
- **[Framestore](../collaborators/framestore.md)** — CGI + real-time puppeting technology (NY + London); key figure: Mike Woods (Head of Studio / ECD Digital)
- **Animal Logic** — Broadcast TV spot animation (Sydney)
- **MLB Advanced Media** — Streaming infrastructure + control room
- **360i** — Digital agency partner
- **Starcom MediaVest Group** — Media agency

## References & Media

### Press
- [Ad Age: "Coca-Cola's Polar Bowl: A Live Second-Screen Super Bowl Experience"](https://adage.com/article/special-report-super-bowl/coca-cola-s-polar-bowl-live-second-screen-super-bowl/232286/)
- [Adweek: Coke Polar Bowl coverage](https://www.adweek.com/brand-marketing/coke-polar-bowl-super-bowl-xlvi/)
- [Campaign Live: full credits](https://www.campaignlive.co.uk)
- [Fast Company: Coke Polar Bowl feature](https://www.fastcompany.com)
- [Smithsonian Magazine: coverage](https://www.smithsonianmag.com)
- [Ad Age (2024): retrospective — campaigns that "hacked" the Super Bowl](https://adage.com)

### Books

### Raw Research
- [Raw research file](../raw/research/wk_coca_cola_polar_bowl_2026-04-07.md)

nodes.title: Coca-Cola: The Letter
nodes.desc: Coca-Cola's global Christmas campaign marking **100 years of Coca-Cola Christmas advertising** — their most expansive festive effort ever, launching in more than **90 markets worldwide**.
nodes.body:
# Coca-Cola: The Letter

## The Campaign

Coca-Cola's global Christmas campaign marking **100 years of Coca-Cola Christmas advertising** — their most expansive festive effort ever, launching in more than **90 markets worldwide**.

The film follows a father who discovers his young daughter's letter to Santa has not been posted. In a desperate bid to make her Christmas wish come true, he embarks on an epic journey — across seas, up cliffs, through jungles — to reach the North Pole before Christmas. The campaign message: *"Give something only you can give."*

Directed by **Taika Waititi** (*Jojo Rabbit*, *Thor: Ragnarok*) — whose home country of New Zealand served as the filming location. Shot over five days in August 2020 during COVID restrictions. In a remarkable logistical achievement, team members in Los Angeles, London, and Madrid reviewed the shoot simultaneously via Zoom. The production staffed entirely with local New Zealand actors and crew.

## Collaborators

**W+K London:**
- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/iain_tait.md)** — Executive Creative Director

nodes.title: Dodge Tent Event — Invisible Monkey

Hall**: *"This event could not be more amazing. Oh, wait. There
nodes.body:
# Dodge Tent Event — Invisible Monkey

## The Campaign

A Dodge "Tent Event" sales promotion offering a free 60-day test-drive. The original TV spot featured a chimpanzee named **Suzy** dressed in an Evel Knievel stuntman outfit, setting off a confetti detonator. Deadpan voiceover by *Dexter* actor **Michael C. Hall**: *"This event could not be more amazing. Oh, wait. There's a monkey. I stand corrected."*

## The Crisis & The Pivot

PETA contacted Dodge in mid-July 2010 to object to the use of a great ape in entertainment. By July 20, Dodge's CEO confirmed the chimp would be removed.

Rather than simply pulling the spot, W+K executed a self-aware pivot: CGI was used to digitally excise Suzy from the footage, leaving an **intentionally obvious empty space** where the chimp had been. Michael C. Hall recorded a new line: *"There's an invisible monkey. Unbelievable."*

The revised "Invisible Monkey" spot aired August 13, 2010 — less than a month after the complaint.

## The Covert Amplification

W+K secretly commissioned **NMA (Next Media Animation World News)** — the Taiwanese internet animation company famous for producing viral news animation segments — to seed the story organically online. NMA produced their animated take-down piece in five hours. The collaboration was kept confidential at the time, making the organic-seeming coverage a designed amplification strategy.

The result: a genuine PR vulnerability became a self-deprecating internet meme, generating far more coverage than the original spot.

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/iain_tait.md)** — Global Interactive Executive Creative Director, W+K Portland
- **Matt Moore** — Creative, W+K Portland (confirmed: uploaded the spot to his personal YouTube channel with first-person framing: *"PETA demanded we take the chimp out of our TV ad — so we did."* Specific role — copywriter or art director — not confirmed in public sources)
- **Michael C. Hall** — Voiceover artist
- **Kristin Starnes** — Brand Communications Director, Dodge (client lead)
- **NMA (Next Media Animation World News)** — Covert amplification (Taiwan)

*Agency co-ECDs at the time: [Mark Fitzloff](../collaborators/mark_fitzloff.md) and [Susan Hoffman](../collaborators/susan_hoffman.md). No further individual W+K creative credits (CD, CW, AD, director, production company) confirmed in available sources.*

nodes.title: Formula 1 Global Rebrand — Engineered Insanity
nodes.desc: Following Liberty Media's $8 billion acquisition of Formula One in January 2017, the new ownership needed to transform a historically closed, niche motorsport property into an open, global entertainment brand — without alienating its existing fanbase. It was F1's first brand overhaul in 23 years.
nodes.body:
# Formula 1 Global Rebrand — Engineered Insanity

## The Objective

Following Liberty Media's $8 billion acquisition of Formula One in January 2017, the new ownership needed to transform a historically closed, niche motorsport property into an open, global entertainment brand — without alienating its existing fanbase. It was F1's first brand overhaul in 23 years.

W+K London were appointed as **Formula One's first-ever global creative agency** in September 2017 — the first time the sport had ever engaged an external agency at this level.

## The Work

### Identity (November 2017)

Led by Richard Turley — W+K London's ECD of Content & Design, recruited by Iain Tait from *Bloomberg Businessweek* — the agency conducted a fundamental deconstruction and rebuild of the F1 brand.

Flamingo Research conducted a 6-month global brand health study that informed the strategic brief, revealing the sport had lost touch with its visceral emotional core in the eyes of fans. Tone of voice was developed separately by Reed Words.

The result was a controversial, hyper-modern visual identity: a new logo revealed at the Abu Dhabi Grand Prix (November 2017) that replaced the 1994 Carter Wong mark. Three custom typefaces — F1 Regular, F1 Turbo, F1 Torque — were designed by typographer Marc Rouault and are now the global typographic standard for F1 communications.

The logo reveal generated:
- **35,000 tweets** on launch day
- Coverage in **1,500+ media outlets**
- Creative Review's **most-read article of 2017**

### "Engineered Insanity" (March 2018)

The first global marketing campaign, launched ahead of the 2018 season opener. The 60-second hero film used six real superfans as cast, physically exposed to wind-tunnel levels of force — visually replicating the violence and G-forces of an actual F1 cockpit — to communicate the sport's elemental brutality to a new audience.

Sound design for the film was recognised at D&AD 2019 and ProMax 2018.

## Collaborators

- **[Iain Tait](../collaborators/iain_tait.md)** — Executive Creative Director
- **Tony Davidson** — Executive Creative Director
- **[James Guy](../collaborators/james_guy.md)** — Executive Producer / Head of Integrated Production, W+K London
- **Richard Turley** — ECD Content & Design (led identity; recruited by Iain Tait from Bloomberg Businessweek)
- **Marc Rouault** — Typographer (designed F1 Regular, F1 Turbo, F1 Torque typefaces)
- **Flamingo Research** — Brand Strategy Research (6-month global study)
- **Reed Words** — Tone of Voice
- **Ellie Norman** — Director of Marketing, Formula One (client; first-ever marketing director role in F1)
- **Sean Bratches** — Managing Director Commercial Operations, Formula One (client)

## Reception & Legacy

### Awards

- **D&AD 2019:** Sound Design recognition (finalist/shortlist)
- **ProMax 2018:** Bronze — Sound Design, "Engineered Insanity"
- *No Cannes Lions confirmed for this work*

### Metrics (2018 season, first full year under new brand)

| Metric | Figure |
|---|---|
| Unique TV viewers | 490.2 million (+10% YoY) |
| Social media followers | 18.5 million (+53%) |
| Tweets at logo reveal | 35,000 |
| Media outlets at logo reveal | 1,500+ |

F1 was reported as the fastest growing major sports property on social media during this period.

### Cultural Legacy

- The rebrand is widely credited as laying the aesthetic and strategic groundwork for F1's global audience explosion — which accelerated further with Netflix's *Drive to Survive* (2019)
- First time F1 ever worked with a global creative agency — a structural shift as significant as the visual one
- Richard Turley's It's Nice That profile (2019) describes Iain Tait's recruitment of him as foundational to the work: *"Iain called me up... Iain runs the London office"*
- Cited in W+K London's 20-year retrospective as one of its milestone projects

## References & Media

nodes.title: Formula 1: NEEEUM
nodes.desc: The third major campaign in the W+K London × F1 account, following the 2017 rebrand and 2018's "Engineered Insanity." NEEEUM gave Formula 1 its **global sonic identity** — a three-second audio logo built from an extreme engineering of a Chemical Brothers track.
nodes.body:
# Formula 1: NEEEUM

## The Campaign

The third major campaign in the W+K London × F1 account, following the 2017 rebrand and 2018's "Engineered Insanity." NEEEUM gave Formula 1 its **global sonic identity** — a three-second audio logo built from an extreme engineering of a Chemical Brothers track.

What began as a licensing conversation became a full collaboration. W+K/F1 approached **Globe** (Universal Music UK's creative consultancy) to license a Chemical Brothers track. Through that process it grew into something else entirely: the band — genuine longstanding F1 fans who had performed at multiple F1 events — agreed to engineer a bespoke remix. They took *"We've Got To Try"* (from their forthcoming album *No Geography*) and accelerated it to **15,000 beats per minute** — matching the 15,000 RPM of a modern F1 car at full speed. The result: the fastest remix ever created. Three seconds of sound.

Globe also co-funded the music video. *No Geography* subsequently reached **No. 2 in the UK charts**, partly on the campaign's reach.

## The Four-Part Campaign

1. **Six-second social films** (launched March 7, 2019) — dog behind a wheel, dog commandeering a rocket
2. **The NEEEUM sonic identity** — "WGTT15000BPM F1 NEEEUM MIX": the 3-second global audio logo
3. **Full music video for "We've Got To Try"** (released March 8, 2019) — directed by Ninian Doff; follows "Girl the Dog" on a mission to complete the impossible
4. **2019 season launch film** (debuted March 11, 2019) — hyping the first Grand Prix in Melbourne using the full Chemical Brothers track

## Metrics

| Metric | Figure |
|---|---|
| Music video organic views (3 weeks) | 1.7 million |
| Global publications covered | 70+ |
| *No Geography* UK chart position | No. 2 |

## The F1 × W+K London Account in Context

NEEEUM was part of a multi-year account transformation commissioned by Liberty Media after they acquired F1 in 2017. CMO **Ellie Norman** tasked W+K London with rebuilding F1 as a global entertainment brand:

| Year | Campaign | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Nov 2017 | "Unleashed" — rebrand | 1,500+ media outlets in 5 days |
| March 2018 | "Engineered Insanity" — first-ever global F1 campaign | +10% unique viewers (490.2M); +53% YoY social to 18.5M followers |
| March 2019 | NEEEUM | 1.7M organic views; 70+ publications; No Geography No. 2 UK |
| Nov 2020 | "Still Rising" — Lewis Hamilton 7th title | — |
| 2021 | "One Begins" — 2022 car livery + launch film | — |

nodes.title: Facebook: Still Going Strong
nodes.desc: W+K London's first TV work for Facebook, made in collaboration with W+K Portland. Part of Facebook's broader **"More Together"** platform — a celebration of what Facebook Groups make possible, even in the hardest times.
nodes.body:
# Facebook: Still Going Strong

## The Campaign

W+K London's first TV work for Facebook, made in collaboration with W+K Portland. Part of Facebook's broader **"More Together"** platform — a celebration of what Facebook Groups make possible, even in the hardest times.

The film was created entirely during COVID-19 lockdown and made using authentic footage sourced from real Facebook Group members, demonstrating that "although we might be apart outside, in Facebook Groups there is still creativity, bravery, and kindness." The spot debuted in the US on **ESPN** during *The Last Dance* documentary, with a 30-second cut for online and a radio version.

## Collaborators
